discoverer: 1999, JINR Dubna / Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory collaboration — confirmed 2012
etymology: Flerov Laboratory of Nuclear Reactions — the division of JINR Dubna where most superheavy elements from 114 onward were synthesized, named after Georgiy Flyorov (1913-1990), the Soviet physicist who founded the laboratory and initiated the Soviet superheavy element research program. Flerov himself had written to Stalin in 1942 urging development of an atomic bomb.

timeline.event: First synthesis of element 114 (flerovium) reported. confirmed 2012
Longest-lived known isotope: 289Fl, t½ = 1.9 seconds. Individual atoms detected by DGFRS (Dubna Gas-Filled Recoil Separator).
IUPAC formally confirms the discovery and approves the name 'flerovium' and symbol 'Fl'. Element 114 officially joins the periodic table.

Flerovium sits at the edge of the predicted island of stability — the region around atomic number 114 and neutron number 184 where nuclear theory predicts unusually stable superheavy nuclei. The 289Fl isotope contains 114 protons and 175 neutrons — approaching but not reaching the predicted magic neutron number of 184. Preliminary chemistry suggests flerovium may be more volatile than lead (its lighter Group 14 congener), behaving more like a noble metal.

Research continues at JINR Dubna, GSI/FAIR, RIKEN, and other facilities. Element 114 represents the current frontier of nuclear synthesis — beyond it lies terra incognita, where nuclear stability predictions become increasingly uncertain and experimental access requires advances in accelerator technology and target production.

story: The Dubna-Livermore collaboration that discovered flerovium (and livermorium, moscovium, and tennessine) was one of the most productive international scientific partnerships of the early 21st century. Dubna supplied the accelerator and expertise; Livermore supplied the actinide target materials from US nuclear stockpiles. The calcium-48 beam — doubly magic and extremely fusion-efficient — was the key to the entire program.
source_note: Discovered by Oganessian group at JINR Dubna in collaboration with Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory (1999). Named after Flerov Laboratory of Nuclear Reactions. Confirmed by IUPAC 2012.
